heading: INFLUISCE SU TUTTE LE MEMBRA
card_summary: This very long chunk (35,971 words) opens the section "Influisce su tutte le membra" (It Influences All the Members) in the Fifth Tone of the Second Canticle of Zorzi's De Harmonia Mundi. Working from natural philosophy — Hippocrates, Aristotle, Galen, and Avicenna on the seminal derivation of seed from the whole body — Zorzi constructs an extended analogy between biological generation and the eternal procession of the Son from the Father, and from that, Christian spiritual regeneration through baptism. He draws on Orphic tradition (Pallas born from the brain of Jupiter), Platonic Symposium cosmology on male/female/androgynous generation in relation to Sun, Earth, and Moon, and a range of Scripture (Psalm 82, Malachi, Matthew, John on Nicodemus, Isaiah on the Prince of Peace, 1 John on sin and divine seed). The role of faith in baptism is explained through Aristotelian and Avicennian theories of imagination, with Moses' account of Jacob and the speckled rods cited as the supreme philosophical precedent. The summary covers the opening portion of this very long section.
essay:
# INFLUISCE SU TUTTE LE MEMBRA
## Pages 1645–1738 (Fifth Tone, Second Canticle)

*Note: This summary covers the opening portion (approximately the first 6,000 words) of a very long section (35,971 words). Later sub-sections are not covered here.*

---

## 1. The Central Argument: From Biological Seed to Divine Procession

The section opens with a programmatic statement of Zorzi's comparative method: in order to explain how the eternal Father and Christ are called "father of all things," it is necessary first to recover the natural philosophers' accounts of biological generation, because in lower realities there are "simulacra and images of divine realities." The analogical movement — from physiology upward to theology, and from theology back down to spiritual practice — governs the entire opening sequence.

Zorzi surveys the ancient debate on the origin of seed: **Hippocrates** locates it in the brain (the foamy, carnal portion); **Aristotle** in the heart as seat of the soul; **Galen** in the liver as seat of digestive and generative power. **Avicenna**, synthesizing all three, argues that seed comes from all three principal organs. A further position — which Zorzi ultimately endorses — holds that seed is secreted from every member of both parents, so that each part of the offspring resembles the corresponding part of the parent:

> *"Et così il cuore è generato dal cuore del generante, l'occhio dall'occhio, la mano dalla mano e così via per ciascun membro, benché la potenza e lo spirito provengano principalmente dal cervello, dal cuore e dal fegato, che sono le membra principali e le più spirituali."*

<cite>p. 1478 (Latin) / p. 1479 (Italian translation)</cite>

---

## 2. Christ as Perfect Image Emanating from All the Father's Attributes

The physiological analogy is immediately applied to Christology. Just as biological seed draws rational power from the brain, affective power from the heart and liver, so **Christ** — "immagine perfetta e sapienza del Padre" — emanated from all that is in the Father: from essence, piety, clemency, justice, goodness, infinity, and the entire Fountain of all goods. As primarily Wisdom, he proceeded principally from the "brain," i.e., the paternal mind, which Zorzi glosses via **Orpheus** and the tradition of the Gentile theologians who said Pallas (true Wisdom) was born from the brain of Jupiter.

> *"Passando dal cuore e dal fegato esso riceve lo spirito dell'amore e dell'affetto. Così anche Cristo, Figlio di Dio, immagine perfetta e sapienza del Padre, emanò da tutte le cose che sono nel Padre, tutte portandole con sé. Perciò disse: Tutte le cose mi sono state date dal Padre mio, poiché da tutte procedette."*

<cite>pp. 1478–1479</cite>

Scripture cited: Matthew 11:27 ("Omnia mihi tradita sunt a Patre meo"). The Logos is identified with the *armarium omnium rerum creatarum* — the repository of all created things — in which all things, visible and invisible, are ideated as in a seminal power. Human beings are generated first from the eternal Father through the Son (who was saturated with the seed of Ideas), and then regenerated through the incarnate Son.

---

## 3. Spiritual Regeneration, Baptism, and the Question of the Mother (Chapter II)

Zorzi turns to the theology of regeneration. Because human beings were made in the image and likeness of God but that image has been disfigured and the likeness lost, regeneration through Christ is necessary. He cites John 3 (the dialogue with Nicodemus) at length: Christ's injunction "Oportet vos nasci denuo" is interpreted through the parallel between water as the principle of first generation (Genesis 1:20, "Producant aquae") and water as the principle of spiritual rebirth.

Zorzi argues that the "water" of John 3:5 is not elemental water but the Son of God himself — "la vera acqua al di là del mondo" — who regenerates together with the Holy Spirit. The sensible elements of baptism (water poured on the head, spoken words) are sacramental signs: one signifies the seed, the other the power of propagation. The Holy Spirit's role is that of the warming, disposing, igneous power.

A lengthy scholastic difficulty is then addressed: natural generation requires a father, a seed, and a mother — but who is the "mother" in spiritual regeneration? Zorzi rejects the standard answer that the Church is this mother, arguing that the Church is merely a relational aggregate of the faithful and cannot simultaneously be the generating mother and the children being generated. He resolves the difficulty by two routes: first, the eternal Father needed no mother for the eternal generation of the Son; second, if one insists that the Church is Christ's bride, this must be understood through the typology of Adam and Eve — the first wife was first a daughter (taken from Adam's rib), then a wife who bore children. The apostolic college, formed from Christ's side (his heartfelt love and the water of grace flowing from his side on the cross), became the first bride and thus the mother of all subsequent Christians. Zorzi cites Paul's letter to the Galatians ("Filioli mei, quos iterum parturio, donec formetur Christus in vobis") as the apostolic paradigm for spiritual maternity.

Sacramental efficacy is mentioned as a disputed question among theologians but Zorzi steers away from it, locating the true power in the generating Father (Christ), of whom the sacramental elements are signs and instruments. Isaiah is quoted twice: "Tu sei nostro padre, tu sei il nostro redentore" (Isaiah 63:16) and "sarà chiamato: Dio forte, principe della pace e padre del mondo a venire" (Isaiah 9:5) — the latter glossed as "father of regeneration."

---

## 4. Faith in Baptism as Imaginative Adhesion (Chapter III)

The third chapter explains why faith is required of the baptized (or their sponsors). Zorzi deploys the Aristotelian and Avicennian doctrine that the offspring resembles the parent through the strong imagination (*imaginatio*) of both the conceiving and the generating party. **Aristotle** and **Avicenna** held that intense imagination can affect not only the imagining subject but external objects as well. **Algazel** (al-Ghazali) and **Galen** are cited in support; Galen famously advised a pregnant woman to fix her eyes on a beautiful statue — of Polycletus or Absalom for beauty, of Caesar or Alexander for martial virtue, of Aristotle or Plato for philosophical excellence.

Zorzi then credits **Moses** as the true philosophical source for this doctrine, citing Genesis 30 (Jacob's use of striped rods in the watering-troughs to produce particoloured offspring):

> *"Questi sapienti appresero forse la loro dottrina dal nostro impeccabile filosofo Mosè, il quale narra che Giacobbe pose dei bastoni nei canali dove si abbeveravano le pecore a seconda di come voleva che diventassero: bianche, nere o maculate."*

<cite>p. 1489</cite>

Faith (*fides*) is thus not mere belief but "firm adhesion and the total concentration of the soul toward the one who sows and regenerates." In infant baptism, the sponsors supply this imaginative adhesion on the child's behalf. Paul's enumeration of faith's works in Hebrews 11 is cited as the supreme scriptural demonstration that firm adhesion to God is the productive principle of all great deeds.

---

## 5. Masculine, Feminine, and Androgynous Spiritual Generation (Chapter IV)

Chapter IV interprets the natural question of sex-determination through mystical and Platonic optics. After reviewing Galen's and Ali Abbas's (Haly Abbas's) view that predominance of male or female seed determines sex, Zorzi turns to **Plato's Symposium**, reading it as a mystical allegory:

> *"Quando l'uomo aderisce al sole – ovvero alla luce e al seme divino – genera un maschio, una femmina se aderisce alla terra e un androgino quando si mescola alla luna."*

<cite>p. 1491 (citing Symposium)</cite>

The Sun = the divine light that illuminates every man and fecundates rational minds (the Johannine Logos); Earth = the animal, sensual part of human nature; Moon = natural philosophy and civic life. Adhering to the divine Sun and receiving Christ's seed produces "masculine" — noble, divine — works. Adhering to the animal-earthly produces effeminate, debased works (with scriptural sanction: those who do so are to be "eliminated from the earth"). Civil engagement alone produces androgynous works, a middle state between divine and animal. Paul is cited: "Qui servit uxori, solicitus est quae sunt mundi" (1 Corinthians 7:33). The baptismal triple renunciation of the devil, the world, and its pomps is then re-read as the ritual enactment of this Platonic choice: total adhesion to the generating Father, removal of everything that might distract. The section closes with 1 John's paradoxical statements that whoever is born of God cannot sin, because "God's seed remains in him" — a text Zorzi notes has not received sufficient theological attention.

---

## Key Technical Terms and Sources
- **Simulacra / imagines**: lower realities as images of divine realities (Neoplatonic-Kabbalistic double register throughout).
- **Virtus seminaria / semen idearum**: seminal power, seed of Ideas in the Logos.
- **Armarium omnium rerum creatarum**: the repository of all created things (the Son as the divine Word containing all ideal patterns).
- **Androginus**: the Platonic-Symposium figure reread as the soul engaged in natural philosophy and civic life, neither fully divine nor fully bestial.
- Sources: Hippocrates, Aristotle (*De anima* II), Galen, Avicenna (*Canon*), Algazel (al-Ghazali), Ali Abbas (Haly Abbas), Orpheus, Plato (*Symposium*), Pseudo-Dionysius (implicit), Scripture throughout (Genesis, Psalms, Malachi, Matthew, John, Isaiah, Galatians, Hebrews, 1 Corinthians, 1 John).
